Dayton Heights Elementary School, another of Ivy Marx's salad bars.
With surprising precision and some direction students enter the cafeteria, split into two lines, and use tongs to help themselves to fruits and vegetables.
Unidentified Child #2: Cherry tomatoes.
This is real cool.
Unidentified Child #3: Kiwi.
There's lots of things to eat and they're all healthy.
Getting the kids to try these foods took some effort.
There were assemblies about nutrition and teacher Marina Morales organized the Harvest of the Month, where different fruits and vegetables are talked about and tasted.
I sometimes do cooking lessons with them.
And we definitely do science and math.
We weigh things, we measure things.
Count seeds.
Yeah, we've done that.
Counting seeds, we talk about attributes like, you know, the color, size of something, comparing them.
But the other thing that I find interesting is the Harvest of the Month produce is also featured in the salad bar.
So when kids saw jicama at the salad bar, they already knew what it was and what it tasted like.
Something, as assistant principal Christopher Etan puts it, only increases their dietary horizons.
When we have a particularly foreign looking object in the salad bar, I'm almost like a peddler on the street.
I know that you would love to try this and sometimes I help children make choices.
I encourage them to be adventurous.
At UCLA, pediatrician Wendy Slusser headed a study which found that after two years students at L.A.
schools with salad bars increased their fruit and vegetable consumption by one full serving a day.
(Pediatrician): And they also displaced fatty foods.

So in other words, they were eating more - they were eating much more of a variety of fruits and vegetables after the salad bar, and then they were also eating lower amounts of cholesterol, significantly, and less saturated fats.
Unidentified Child #4: I picked some apples and some oranges.

Unidentified Child #6: Definitely try new things - cucumbers, tomatoes and lettuce.
Choices you might begin to hear more and more as nationwide school districts like Los Angeles work to offer kids healthier lunches, including salad bars.

In a few hours, Israel will release 26 Palestinian men from prison.

They're being freed before they finish serving their full sentences.

Riyad Aweidah knows what it's like to be in an Israeli prison.

In Israel, freeing Palestinians who killed Israelis is creating a political firestorm.
Israel's economy minister, Naftali Bennett, called it immoral.
He introduced a measure over the weekend to stop future prisoner releases.
Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's party voted down the proposal, but the split reflects rising tensions within Israel's fragile coalition.
Netanyahu agreed to release the prisoners as a concession to the Palestinian Authority in order to restart peace talks.
Now three months in, Israel has said little about how negotiations are going.
